How I Think

The thinking behind the work

01
I go upstream first

Most briefs present a symptom as the problem. A brand that can't grow its audience doesn't have a content problem — it usually has a positioning problem. A brand that keeps spending on advertising without traction doesn't have a media problem — it has a clarity problem. I don't stop at the first explanation that makes sense. I stay with the question until something truer begins to show itself.

02
Brand strategy is a sales problem

When brands don't solve their brand strategy problem, they'll keep spending on advertising indefinitely and never be able to stop. The spend becomes a substitute for clarity. Brand strategy, done properly, reduces that dependency. It makes a brand strong enough to be remembered without constantly shouting. I find that solving the positioning problem is always the most efficient commercial decision.

03
I ride the waves

Strategy is always moving. I maintain a deliberate practice I call riding the waves — studying business and political climates, tracking which brands are taking the largest market shares and understanding why, reading AI industry reports, following cultural shifts. Not because all of it applies to every brief, but because the strategist who can only see today's problem will always be one step behind. I want to be immediately relevant for a brand trying to crack tomorrow's world.

04
I prove the thinking by building

I built City on a Hill — a youth development community — from zero, with no ad spend, no existing audience, and no safety net. Just brand strategy, positioning, and the discipline to sustain it. It now reaches over 1,000 young people across Nigeria and the UK. That was a deliberate challenge to myself: could the thinking alone build something real? I believe the answer is yes.

Selected Work

Strategy that thinks

FMCG  ·  Nigeria Market Entry Brand & Market Entry Strategy

Sankofa Natural Spices

"How do you enter a market as the healthier choice without making health the reason to choose you?"

View full strategy document ↗

The Brief

Sankofa Natural Spices wanted to enter Nigeria and compete with seasoning giants — positioning as the first mass-market, non-MSG, no-salt, healthy spice option in the market.

The Challenge

Jollof wars between Nigeria and Ghana meant a Ghanaian brand entering Nigeria carried immediate cultural risk. Nigerians have intensely loyal taste palates — Knorr, Maggi, and Terra have decades of deep penetration. How does a new, healthier option earn genuine consideration without triggering defensiveness or coming across as clinical?

My Approach

Sankofa could not arrive waving a Ghanaian flag — that is an Achilles heel, not a differentiator. And it could not lead with health as a banner — Nigerians do not want to be told their cooking is wrong. Through research, I found the real emotional truth: Nigerians want guilt reduction, not guilt elimination. They want to eat their cake and have it. So Sankofa enters not as a replacement but as the brand that completes the pot — meeting Nigerians exactly where they are, respecting their cultural truth, and offering something that suits both ego and health at the same time.

The Big Idea

Crown Your Pot. Not "switch your seasoning" — crown it. Elevate what you already cook. Sankofa is not asking Nigerians to change how they cook. It is asking them to add one thing: a seasoning that is completely on their side. Functionally, it adds to what you already cook. Emotionally, your cooking deserves better — not different, better.

View full strategy document ↗

The Problem

The fashion industry is saturated with premium cultural pieces that look runway-ready but live nowhere near everyday life. Bags are either too stylish or too pricey — and rarely functional. As Nigerians increasingly embrace fast fashion for its practicality, cultural pieces are being left behind. Meanwhile, the collaborative economy across pan-African fashion is almost non-existent. It is more expensive to import from Kenya to Nigeria than from China to Nigeria — a genuinely perplexing reality that keeps African craft inaccessible to the very African consumers who should own it.

The Challenge

Cultural differences still stand and people's purses still speak. Functional pieces demand higher quality materials and more capital. And there is a perception problem: how do you get someone to believe in a raffia and leather tote bag before they have already decided it is too expensive? On the business side, how do you price for cultural integrity and still break even?

My Approach

I designed both the business strategy and the brand strategy simultaneously — because the perception problem and the business model problem were the same problem. The solution was a two-product architecture: INTERSECT Original, fashion-forward and imported from Kenyan artisans or made by Nigerian craftspeople as pure cultural statements; and INTERSECT Functional, made in Nigeria to meet practical standards of width, depth, and everyday use. On the brand side, I positioned INTERSECT as a pop culture brand with a storytelling soul — weaving in the narratives of the artisans themselves, making the brand feel genuinely pan-African rather than aesthetically African. The long-term vision is bigger than bags: a proof of concept for scalable economic collaboration across the continent, and eventually a case study for policy conversations about intra-African trade.

The Big Idea

Wear Your Continent. Not a slogan about fashion — a statement about identity, pride, and the quiet economic revolution that happens when African women choose African craft. Every piece is a vote for a world where the roads between us are shorter than they told us.

Brand Expression

View full case study ↗

The Brief

Oduko is a web app designed for the holistic upskilling of Black women globally — built across six development pathways covering everything from career and finances to wellness and identity. The founder wanted to speak to two audiences simultaneously: the woman seeking growth and healing, and the woman who is herself a trainer or coach. She also wanted to communicate all six pillars of the platform from the very beginning.

The Challenge

The strategic problem was one of clarity. A new brand cannot afford to divide the minds of the people it is trying to reach. Launching with two audiences and six value propositions at once would create a brand that feels like everything and lands as nothing. The harder truth was that a tech product — no matter how thoughtfully designed — will not earn trust without first earning belonging. The product needed a soul before it needed a feature list.

The Tension

The founder's instinct was to lead with everything — the full platform, all six pillars, both audiences. My instinct was to lead with one thing: community. We held that tension honestly. She wanted completeness; I wanted clarity. What we eventually agreed on was a middle path that neither of us had to abandon our convictions for.

My Approach

I recommended that Oduko enter the market not as a tech product but as a space with a soul — a women's community you want to belong to before you ever know it is an app. Sell the feeling first. Earn the trust. Then reveal the infrastructure. This meant the launch strategy could not open with features or pathways. It had to open with a woman, a live conversation, a sense of warmth and recognition. The brand DNA I developed defined Oduko's tone as pop-culture fluent and community-driven — so that learning never feels clinical, and growth never feels like homework.

The Big Idea

A space with a soul. Before Oduko is a platform, it is a place. Before it is a product, it is a feeling of being seen, challenged, and celebrated by women who understand exactly where you are. The six pillars exist — but they are revealed through relationship, not through a features page. The brand enters as community. The product follows.

View full brand DNA ↗

The Brief

Ducros — a heritage seasoning brand known for Thyme and Curry — had flavoured Nigerian kitchens for generations but went dark on advertising for nearly two decades. Returning online during the festive season, the campaign needed to re-establish Ducros digitally across Meta and TikTok, drive awareness-to-sales conversion, and strengthen its legacy positioning as the original taste-maker. I served as both strategist and account manager, overseeing execution across influencer marketing, digital content, and OOH billboard advertising with a total campaign spend of ₦16M including influencer management.

The Challenge

The category was intensely competitive. Gino had Hilda Baci and the Guinness World Record Jollof moment. Knorr had a full creator programme. Maggi had the August Meeting and Bole Festival activations. Terra had BBNaija. Ducros was returning with no recent brand presence, no ambassador, and no digital footprint into a category where every major competitor was actively spending and winning cultural moments. The risk was that a generic "we're back" campaign would be invisible — or worse, feel like a brand catching up rather than a brand that never truly left.

My Approach

The insight came from consumer research: urban Nigerians specifically seek out Ducros Curry for its fragrance, even when it costs more. That loyalty is driven by sensory memory and perceived quality — Ducros by McCormick is known globally for consistent, high-grade spice. The silence wasn't absence. It was maturity. The strategy repositioned the silence itself — not as something to apologise for, but as the quiet confidence of a brand that had been letting quality speak all along. This became the emotional architecture of the campaign: Ducros didn't go anywhere. It was always in the kitchen. You just weren't listening.

The Big Idea

Seasoned With Time. This idea bridges Ducros' heritage with its renewed relevance — celebrating the brand's enduring place in Nigerian kitchens, the spice that has flavoured both our food and our memories. From grandma's pot to today's party rice, Ducros has seasoned our lives. It is not a comeback. It is a homecoming.

View full strategy document ↗

The Brief

SANU Coin is Nigeria's first asset-backed digital bullion platform — allowing users to buy, hold, and physically redeem gold and silver. The campaign needed to position SANU as a credible, calm alternative to speculative crypto and unstable savings apps, targeting professionals, business owners, and emerging investors across Lagos.

The Challenge

Nigeria's consumer is living through persistent inflation anxiety, currency volatility, and deep distrust of "paper promises." At the same time, over-hyped financial products have made the average Nigerian investor defensive — they've been promised returns before. The category tension was acute: people understand gold and silver as stores of value culturally, but most digital finance advertising feels like speed, noise, and speculation. SANU needed to occupy the opposite space entirely — without being so institutional that it felt inaccessible.

My Approach

The strategic diagnosis was clear: SANU must behave like financial infrastructure, not another app. That meant stripping out all hype language, all urgency mechanics, all get-rich-fast framing. I built the strategy around four pillars — Trust (proof of custody and origin), Access (the ₦100 entry point as a daily sovereignty ritual), Tangibility (buy digitally, redeem physically), and Tone (institutional, minimalist, quietly confident). The media plan blended OOH in Lagos as a legitimacy and trust layer — billboards in Lekki, Ikoyi, Third Mainland Bridge, BRT branding — with a digital funnel moving audiences from legitimacy through education to conversion and retention. OOH was not decoration. It was proof.

The Big Idea

Real Metal, Real Ownership. In a market tired of hype and paper promises, SANU positions bullion as the calm, credible alternative — financial infrastructure, not another app. The idea reframes gold ownership from elite privilege to everyday access: you can start from ₦100 and build real, sovereign-grade value in small, consistent actions. And the ultimate trust signal is physical reality — buy digitally, redeem physically, so what you own is provable, tangible, and protected.

View full strategy document ↗

The Brief

Brydge Finance is a B2B platform simplifying intra-African trade — offering seamless payments, compliance support, trade finance access, and market penetration services for importers and exporters. With operations in Kano and Lagos and expansion targets across Cameroon, Ivory Coast, Benin, Togo, and Kenya, Brydge needed a full marketing strategy to build brand awareness, generate inbound leads, and establish authority in a category most African businesses don't yet trust.

The Challenge

Most African businesses still import from China. Intra-African trade feels risky, unfamiliar, or underdeveloped — not because the opportunity doesn't exist, but because the infrastructure of trust doesn't. Brydge's real competitors weren't FrontEdge or Cellulant. They were habit, informal networks, and fear of fraud. A platform-first entry into this market would fail. Brydge needed to earn belief before it could earn users. The additional complexity: the target audience was three distinct psychographic groups — importers, exporters, and financiers — each with entirely different pain points, purchase behaviours, and trust triggers.

My Approach

I built a full market playbook rather than a campaign. The insight driving everything: Brydge must not only offer the tools but also inspire belief that this model can work, and work profitably. That meant the strategy couldn't be led by product features — it had to be led by human truth and community. I developed three distinct audience personas — Chuka (importer), Misturah (exporter), and David (financier) — and built separate messaging ladders for each, phased across three campaign stages: Visibility and Trust, Engagement and Education, and Lead Conversion and Localised Scale. The multilingual dimension was non-negotiable: Hausa for Kano radio, French for Francophone West Africa, Swahili optionally for Kenya. The brand line — Your Trade Padi — humanised a complex B2B platform into a relationship, using deeply Nigerian slang to make Brydge feel like a partner rather than a system.

The Big Idea

Your Trade Padi. Not "your trade platform" — your Padi. In Nigerian vernacular, a padi is someone you trust, someone who has your back, someone who shows up when the deal gets complicated. Brydge positions itself not as infrastructure but as the most reliable relationship in the room. The idea was also translated across five languages — Ore (Yoruba), Oyim (Igbo), Aboki (Hausa), Pote (French), Budda (Swahili) — so that every market heard it in a voice that felt like their own.

What Made This Strategy Different

The depth of this work went beyond positioning. I conducted a full SEO audit revealing Brydge's domain authority of 2 and only one backlink — and built a 6-month SEO and AEO roadmap to address it. I designed a tiered budget plan at three investment levels (₦5M, ₦10M, ₦20M quarterly) so the client could grow into the strategy without abandoning it. I mapped offline activation locations — Alaba, ASPAMDA, Balogun, Kano Economic City, Ariaria Market, Gikomba — because in markets where trust is built face-to-face, digital alone is insufficient. The full strategy covered brand positioning, channel mix, content pillars, influencer tiers, phased messaging ratios, community growth mechanics, and a complete KPI framework. It is the most comprehensive strategy document I have built to date.

Beyond Strategy

What else I bring

◈
Brand Identity Design

Strong eye for visual identity — colour, typography, layout, and brand expression. I have designed brand decks, presentations, and strategy documents that hold their own aesthetically. Design is not separate from strategy for me; it is how strategy becomes felt.

◉
AI for Marketing

Prompt engineering, AI animation, and image generation using Claude, ChatGPT, Kimi, and Flow. I trained an entire agency team on AI content production and produced an original AI-animated series. I use AI to move faster without losing quality.

◎
Canva — Advanced

Agency-level strategy decks, brand websites, and presentation systems inside Canva. Three live websites built with Canva Code. If it needs to look exceptional, I build it myself.

◆
Writing & Storytelling

Strategy documents, brand narratives, creative briefs, and content — all with the same commitment to clarity and voice. Writing is how I think out loud. The strongest ideas in my strategies are always expressed in a single line.

◇
Personal Brand Building

Built my own personal brand from zero — content strategy across LinkedIn, Instagram, TikTok, and Facebook; ManyChat funnels; coaching products on Mainstack and Selar; 100K+ cumulative impressions. I teach it because I live it.

○
Training & Development

Led 15+ teams and trained over 60 young professionals in marketing, media, and AI strategy. Designed and ran the QVT Youth Mentorship Initiative from scratch. Built curriculum and a near-peer coaching model at City on a Hill.

About

The mind behind it

I got into brand strategy through a rabbit hole that started with watching an English designer named Abi Connick talk about how strategy made her work distinct. That observation — that thinking is what separates memorable brands from forgettable ones — has never left me.

I went deep. Lindsay Marsh. Chris Do. Anneli Hansson. A brand report by Victor Fatanmi on Nigerian startups that genuinely changed how I saw market competition. I became fascinated by the planning that goes into building a brand that earns a permanent place in a person's mind — and I began to understand that strategy is upstream of everything.

My subsequent roles weren't always called brand strategy. Communications. Social media. Media planning. Marketing management. But in every one of them, I applied brand strategy even when it wasn't asked of me. I couldn't help it. How a brand is positioned and perceived is upstream of conversion, loyalty, and distinctiveness. It all begins there.

With six years of experience across FMCG, fintech, pharma, sports, fashion, and edtech — and a youth community I built from zero to 2,000 people using nothing but brand thinking — I have come to believe that the right strategy doesn't just help a brand compete. It changes what competing means.
